pages.raw_text:
A. w. Perley, inspector of tracks
and safety appliances for the state
railroad commission, with head
quarters at Olympia, is in the city.
He has just returned from an in
spection of the Kennewick wreck,
of which he will say little, as no re-
port has yet been submitted to the
n1n1ission. The office occupied by
Mr.. Perley was created at the last
session of the legislature for the
benefit of railway employees and
since, April 1 Mr.. Perley has made
an extensive record Of inspections.


The contemplated action taken by
the commission to compel the n. p.
to build double track on either
side of the Cascade tunnel for the
purpose of relieving traffic conges-
tion has been abandoned. as this
road has reduced it's tonnage to
such an extent that an improvement
Is looked for. After close inspection
Mr.. Perley attributes the main
cause of the congestion to the fault
of the operators in keeping trains
standing upon sidings an unneces-
sary length of time. At the pres
ent time a train passes through this
tunnel every SO minutes.


Mr.. Perley's first complaint TO
the commission called attention to
disabled cars hauled by means Of
chain couplings past places where
repairs could have been made, in
violation of both state and federal
laws. He found cars at Ellens-
burg, chained together, ready to be
hauled to Tacoma for repairs, it
being a common practice Of the y.
P. TO haul cars from near the shops
at Pasco over the mountains to
Tacoma.


The new laws passed at the last
session of the legislature will be
enforced by Mr. Perley June IA.
One restricts all railway companies
throughout the state from working
their men more than 16 consecu-
tive hours. Another requires that
all fagmen must be able to speak
English.


Mr.. Perley was for % years an
o. r. & N. engineer. He is max.
ing an official inspection of tracks
and equipment in this city.
